title: Debugging, Review, and Adversarial Prompting · Guided Eval Studio
summary: Diagnose a failing build and review the repair against a base branch. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of an evidence-first debug and review playbook while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Capture a minimal reproduction, exact error, environment, and last-known-good boundary before repair.
Rank competing hypotheses and choose discriminating checks.
Prompt for the smallest cause-level repair and a regression test.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model
steps.instruction:
Use a changed workshop case derived from this module: prompt debug review. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result.

Use a changed workshop case derived from this module: prompt debug review. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result. What must be established before implementation?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for capture a minimal reproduction, exact error, environment, and last-known-good boundary before repair.

content.text: Capture a minimal reproduction, exact error, environment, and last-known-good boundary before repair. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Check preconditions, perform the operation in an inspectable order, then verify the result and a boundary case.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Use a changed worked example case derived from this module: prompt debug review. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result. A defensible solution applies Reproduction, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to an evidence-first debug and review playbook.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact
steps.instruction: Append a contract headed "Evidence: prompt-adversarial-cases-fx3p07" with concrete Goal, Context, Constraints, Output, and Done when sections tailored to this case.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Adversarial Cases into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
Evidence: prompt-adversarial-cases-fx3p07
Goal: [replace with one observable outcome]
Context: [replace with decision-relevant facts]
Constraints: [replace with authority and safety boundaries]
Output: [replace with the artifact and evidence shape]
Done when: [replace with changed-case acceptance criteria]
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

stimulus.title: Adversarial Cases failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Normal examples predict behavior under adversarial input.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Test prompts and code against misleading context, malformed inputs, and instruction injection.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Adversarial Cases condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model
steps.instruction: Write a concise evidence note that explains when Adversarial Cases applies, what can fail, and how the changed case was verified.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Test prompts and code against misleading context, malformed inputs, and instruction injection.
